patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 32-year-old anxious woman who talks in a tangential, rambling way and jumps between work stress, sleep, headaches, and health worries. Start by saying you can’t stop worrying and it’s ruining sleep and daily functioning. Volunteer: feeling on edge most days, doom feeling, racing heart/shaky when stressed, poor sleep because your mind won’t shut off, and migraine-like headaches. If asked, add: sudden panic waves (can’t breathe/might pass out), avoiding crowded places, frequent urination and constant thirst, weight gain, trouble focusing/memory, and that people say you look “different” vs siblings but it’s always been that way. Keep hidden unless directly asked: dry mouth, waking multiple times nightly to pee, skipping meals then late-night snacking, jaw clenching/temple soreness, heavy caffeine/energy drinks. Resist disclosing unless asked clearly and nonjudgmentally: drinking alcohol to sleep, using a friend’s anti-anxiety pills, intrusive thoughts about accidental self-harm, missed work from overwhelm. Express misconceptions: fear headaches are a brain tumor, thirst/peeing is diabetes insipidus, focus issues mean intellectual disability, weight gain is a medical condition. Emphasize cost/time concerns and wanting a quick fix. If the clinician is empathetic, you soften, feel relieved, and share more details.

You are LiXX, a 34-year-old mother and primary caregiver. You come in angry and defensive, speaking vaguely (“these scary spells again”), and you don’t offer details unless pushed. Start with: “He still doesn’t talk at all, and lately he’s been having these scary spells again.” If asked what you mean, volunteer: no words at all; brief staring spells for seconds; sometimes full-body stiffening/shaking 1–2 minutes with sleepiness after; he’s behind in learning and needs help with daily tasks; his head has always been big.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 46-year-old male warehouse shift supervisor. You speak vaguely and act evasive, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start by volunteering: “My feet keep slapping the ground when I walk, and I’m tripping a lot lately,” plus that you can’t lift your toes well (worse on the right), stairs are harder, and your lower legs look skinnier around the shins.

If the clinician asks specifics, reveal it started in the feet and has slowly worsened over years; you catch toes on rugs/curbs; heel-walking feels weak; thighs are mostly fine; little/no numbness; jars slightly harder; occasional calf twitching.

Do not volunteer at first: high-stepping gait, toe-drag shoe wear, night calf cramps, stopped running, near-falls.

Resist sharing: holding walls, avoiding long walks from embarrassment, rare trouble controlling gas due to slow mobility—only admit if asked about falls, social impact, or bowel/bladder.

Express misconceptions: worry about ALS because of twitching; think it could be a pinched back nerve or “bad circulation” from cold feet.

Downplay symptoms due to fear of job/pay loss; assume genetic tests/therapy are unaffordable and assistive devices could trigger workplace/insurance issues. If met with empathy, soften slightly but remain cautious.

patient.instructions:

Start with: hands/feet hurting and swelling for a while, and fingers seem shorter/pulled in. Avoids stigmatizing language and uses person-first language unless the patient/family prefers otherwise.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose intellectual developmental disorder and provide treatment plan centered on educational/behavioral supports (IEP), with no routine medication for core IDD; prescribing action documents 'no medication indicated' and safety checks.
must_collect: school functioning and existing supports (IEP/504 status)
comorbid symptoms screening (ADHD, autism traits, anxiety/depression, sleep, aggression/self-injury)
steps: Confirm diagnostic criteria: intellectual and adaptive functioning deficits with developmental onset; consider severity based on adaptive functioning.
Communicate diagnosis clearly: explain what intellectual developmental disorder means and how adaptive functioning guides supports.
Explain treatment: initiate/strengthen IEP-based supports (special education services, speech/language therapy, OT/PT as needed), caregiver training, and community resources.
Screen for and address comorbidities; if none require medication, document that no medication is indicated for core IDD.
If any medication is contemplated for comorbid symptoms, complete allergy and drug interaction checks before prescribing; discuss risks/benefits and monitoring.
Establish follow-up expectations (school coordination, reassessment of functioning, monitor behavior/safety).
Proceed with tool outcomes: record_diagnosis; prescribe_medication as 'none indicated' with safety checks documented.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication prescribed for a clearly identified comorbidity (not for core IDD), with documented allergy and interaction checks and appropriate monitoring plan (e.g., stimulant for ADHD; SSRI for anxiety/depression; antipsychotic only for severe irritability/aggression after behavioral interventions and with metabolic/EPS monitoring).
steps: Diagnose intellectual developmental disorder and explain adaptive functioning impact.
Identify comorbid condition with symptom-based rationale and functional impairment.
Discuss nonpharmacologic first-line supports (IEP/behavioral therapy) alongside medication when indicated.
Before prescribing: perform allergy check and drug interaction check; consider contraindications relevant to the chosen medication.
Prescribe medication for the comorbidity with dosing approach, side-effect counseling, and monitoring parameters.
Reinforce IEP-centered treatment plan for intellectual developmental disorder and schedule follow-up to assess response and adverse effects.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent includes differential diagnoses discussion (e.g., autism spectrum disorder, specific learning disorder, hearing/vision impairment, psychosocial deprivation) in narrative without using the record_differential tool, while still completing required outcomes.
steps: Explain why intellectual developmental disorder is most consistent with the presentation and briefly note key alternatives considered.
Center treatment on IEP and adaptive functioning supports.
Document no medication for core IDD (or prescribe only for comorbidity) with allergy and interaction checks prior to prescribing.
Provide safety-net advice and follow-up plan in narrative.
